section.title: Haman's Plot Against the Jews
section.sectionRationale: The danger arrives. Haman the Agagite is promoted to second in the empire, and when Mordecai the Jew will not bow, Haman's wounded pride swells into a plan to exterminate every Jew in the kingdom. He casts lots for the date, buys a genocidal decree from a careless king, and seals it with the royal signet. An ancient enmity, Amalek against Israel, flares into a death sentence for the whole covenant people, published across the empire in a single day.
section.thesis: One man's pride, backed by an old satanic hatred of God's people and an irreversible pagan law, sets a genocide in motion; the covenant people are condemned to death, and only the God who is never named can turn it back.
section.frameNote: Four movements: Haman exalted and Mordecai unbowed; the casting of the lot; Haman purchasing a decree of destruction; and the death decree sent through the empire.

verses.text: After these events, King Xerxes honored Haman son of Hammedatha, the {{agagi|Agagite}}, elevating him to a position above all the princes who were with him.
All the royal servants at the king’s gate bowed down and paid {{shachah|homage}} to Haman, because the king had commanded that this be done for him. But Mordecai would not bow down or pay homage.
Then the royal servants at the king’s gate asked Mordecai, “Why do you disobey the command of the king?”
Day after day they warned him, but he would not comply. So they reported it to Haman to see whether Mordecai’s behavior would be tolerated, since he had told them he was a Jew.
When Haman saw that Mordecai would not bow down or pay him homage, he was filled with rage.

And when he learned the identity of Mordecai’s people, he scorned the notion of laying hands on Mordecai alone. Instead, he sought to destroy all of Mordecai’s people, the Jews, throughout the kingdom of Xerxes.

units.label: The lot is cast
units.note: a date chosen by chance, ruled from heaven

verses.text: In the twelfth year of King Xerxes, in the first month, the month of Nisan, the {{pur|Pur}} (that is, the lot) was cast before Haman to determine a day and month. And the lot fell on the twelfth month, the month of Adar.

units.label: Haman buys a genocide
units.note: a lie, a bribe, and a signet ring

verses.text: Then Haman informed King Xerxes, “There is a certain people scattered and dispersed among the peoples of every province of your kingdom. Their laws are different from everyone else’s, and they do not obey the king’s laws. So it is not in the king’s best interest to tolerate them.
If it pleases the king, let a decree be issued to destroy them, and I will deposit ten thousand talents of silver into the royal treasury to pay those who carry it out.”
So the king removed the signet ring from his finger and gave it to Haman son of Hammedatha, the Agagite, the enemy of the Jews.
“Keep your money,” said the king to Haman. “These people are given to you to do with them as you please.”

units.label: The death decree goes out
units.note: a whole people condemned in a day

verses.text: On the thirteenth day of the first month, the royal scribes were summoned and the order was written exactly as Haman commanded the royal satraps, the governors of each province, and the officials of each people, in the script of each province and the language of every people. It was written in the name of King Xerxes and sealed with the royal signet ring.

And the letters were sent by couriers to each of the royal provinces with the order to destroy, kill, and annihilate all the Jews, young and old, women and children, and to plunder their possessions on a single day, the thirteenth day of Adar, the twelfth month.
A copy of the text of the edict was to be issued in every province and published to all the people, so that they would be ready on that day.
The couriers left, spurred on by the king’s command, and the edict was issued in the citadel of Susa. Then the king and Haman sat down to drink, but the city of Susa was in confusion.

agagi.gloss: Agagite, marking Haman as a descendant, or at least a symbolic heir, of Agag, king of the Amalekites, 1 Samuel 15. Amalek was Israel's ancient enemy, sworn against by God, Exodus 17:16. Mordecai, a Benjamite son of Kish, stands in the line of Saul, so the old war between Israel and Amalek flares again in a Persian court.

shachah.gloss: To bow down, prostrate, or pay homage, the same verb used for worship. Mordecai's refusal to bow is likely more than rudeness: to prostrate before an Agagite, the sworn enemy of God's people, would betray his identity and his God. His stand triggers Haman's rage and the whole crisis.

pur.gloss: The Pur, a lot or die cast to choose a lucky day, an Akkadian loanword the narrator explains as the lot. Haman trusts chance to time his slaughter, but Proverbs 16:33 says the lot is cast into the lap, yet its every decision is from the LORD. The feast of Purim will take its name from this very word.

basic.context: The threat now takes shape. The king promotes Haman to the highest place under the throne and commands everyone to bow to him. Mordecai the Jew will not. Enraged, and despising the idea of punishing one man, Haman resolves to wipe out Mordecai's entire people. He casts lots to pick the date, then sells the king a lie, a troublesome people who do not obey the laws, and offers a fortune for permission to destroy them. The king hands over his signet ring and waves off the money. A decree goes out across the empire: on one day, kill every Jew, young and old, women and children. Then the king and Haman sit down to drink while the city reels.
basic.hermeneutics: This is not simply court intrigue; it is the latest round in an ancient war. Haman is an Agagite, heir of Amalek, Israel's sworn enemy; Mordecai descends from Saul's line. The narrator wants us to feel the deep, almost cosmic hatred behind the plot, and to notice how casually monstrous evil can become when pride meets power. The pressing question is God's hiddenness at its darkest: where is He when a decree of genocide is sealed and the king toasts it with wine? The book's answer, still unspoken, is that even the lot falls under His hand.
basic.originalLanguages: The word Agagite ties Haman to Agag the Amalekite, and the old command that Israel and Amalek would be at war from age to age. Mordecai refuses to bow, shachah, the verb for homage and worship, because bowing to this enemy would deny his people and his God. And Haman casts the Pur, the lot, trusting chance, though Scripture insists the lot's every outcome is from the LORD. From that word Pur the Jews will one day name their festival of rescue, Purim.
basic.typology: Haman is a vivid picture of the serpent's ongoing war on the seed of the woman, the recurring attempt to destroy the covenant people and, through them, cut off the promised Messiah. The condemned Jews, sentenced to death by an irreversible law they cannot escape, foreshadow all humanity under the sentence of sin. Their only hope is a deliverer who can overturn a doom that looks final.
basic.christ: Behind Haman stands the ancient enemy who has always sought to destroy God's people and stop the coming of Christ, from Pharaoh's slaughter of the Hebrew boys to Herod's slaughter at Bethlehem. Every such plot fails, because God guards the line of the Messiah. And the death sentence hanging over the Jews, an unchangeable law demanding their destruction, mirrors the law that condemns us all; only in Christ is an irreversible sentence answered by a greater deliverance.
basic.god: God is never named, and the horror is left to stand: a genocide decreed, the enemy and the king drinking together. Yet even here God is not absent. The lot that sets the date falls, unknown to Haman, on the far end of the year, giving time for rescue. The very irreversibility that dooms the Jews will trap Haman too. God rules the pride of men and the fall of the dice, and He will not let His people be destroyed.
basic.crossReferences: Exodus 17 records the LORD's war with Amalek from generation to generation. Proverbs 16 says the lot is cast, but its decision is from the LORD. Genesis 3 promises enmity between the serpent and the woman's seed. And Revelation 12 shows the dragon waging war on God's people, always defeated.
basic.oneStory: An old satanic hatred, embodied in an Agagite's pride, wins a death sentence for the whole covenant people. It is the serpent's endless war on the seed of the woman, and it looks, for now, unstoppable. But the God who rules even the lot has already timed His rescue, and the line that leads to the Messiah will not be cut off.

context.literary: The chapter turns the story from rise to peril, from banquets to a death decree. It builds through Haman's swelling rage, the ominous casting of the lot, the slick lie to the king, and the chilling detail of the edict, young and old, women and children. It ends on a deliberately jarring image: the king and Haman drinking while Susa is thrown into confusion, exposing the callousness at the empire's heart.
context.historical: Persian administration really did issue decrees in many scripts and languages, sealed with the royal signet, and carried by a famous courier system. Ten thousand talents of silver was an almost unimaginable sum. Casting lots to find a favorable day was common in the ancient Near East. Haman's charge that the Jews' laws were different exploited a genuine tension between the covenant people and pagan society.
context.canonical: Haman as an Agagite and Mordecai as a Benjamite son of Kish deliberately recall Saul's failure to destroy Agag in 1 Samuel 15. The old conflict with Amalek, and behind it the serpent's war on God's people, resurfaces. This is one more attempt in Scripture to annihilate the covenant line, and like all the others it is aimed, ultimately, at preventing the coming of the Messiah.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To expose the depth and the ancient roots of the threat against God's people, to show how pride and irreversible law combine into a death sentence, and to set the stage for a rescue that only a hidden, sovereign God could accomplish.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The chapter describes Haman's evil, the king's carelessness, and a monstrous decree; it commends none of them. Mordecai's refusal to bow is presented as faithful, but the abiding lessons are the reality of evil's hatred of God's people and the sovereignty of God over even the casting of lots.

debates.question: Why would Mordecai not bow to Haman?

views.label: A matter of faith and identity
views.case: Bowing to an Agagite, the sworn enemy of God's people, would compromise Mordecai's loyalty to God and betray his people; the text links his refusal directly to his being a Jew, suggesting a religious and covenantal stand, not mere stubbornness.

views.label: A clash of ancient enmity
views.case: Others stress the Saul-Agag, Israel-Amalek backdrop: Mordecai the Benjamite will not do homage to the heir of Amalek, so the refusal is charged with the old, God-ordained hostility between the two peoples rather than a general principle about bowing to officials.

debates.bearing: Both readings honor the text: Mordecai's refusal is an act of covenant faithfulness that reignites an ancient war, and God will use even Haman's murderous response to deliver His people.

study.originalLanguages: The chapter is framed by an ancient feud. Haman is the Agagite, heir of Agag the Amalekite, and Mordecai a Benjamite son of Kish stands in Saul's line, so their clash reopens the LORD's declared war with Amalek. Mordecai will not bow, shachah, the verb for homage and worship, because prostrating before this enemy would deny his people and his God. Haman casts the Pur, the lot, staking his slaughter on chance, though Scripture insists the lot's every fall is from the LORD, and unknown to Haman the die lands eleven months out, leaving room for rescue. From that grim word Pur the Jews will name Purim, their feast of deliverance.

typology.type: Haman the Agagite seeking to destroy the covenant people
typology.fulfillment: The serpent's ongoing war on the seed of the woman, aimed at cutting off the Messiah, always defeated, Genesis 3:15 and Revelation 12:1-6.

typology.type: The Jews condemned by an irreversible law
typology.fulfillment: All people under the just sentence of sin, from which only a greater deliverance can rescue, Romans 6:23 and Galatians 3:13.

typology.type: The lot cast to time the slaughter
typology.fulfillment: The sovereignty of God, who rules the outcome of the lot and overturns the plans of the wicked, Proverbs 16:33.

study.christ: This chapter shows the serpent's hand more clearly than any other in the book. Behind Haman's pride is the ancient, satanic drive to destroy the people of God and, through them, prevent the promised Christ, the same enmity seen in Pharaoh's murdered infants and Herod's slaughter at Bethlehem, Matthew 2:16. Every attempt fails, because God defends the line of the Messiah. The sentence of death that Haman wins, an unchangeable law demanding the destruction of an entire people, is a dark mirror of the law that condemns all sinners. Where the doom looks final, Scripture points to the Deliverer whose greater and irreversible work of salvation swallows up the sentence of death, so that in Christ the condemned are given life, Colossians 2:14-15.
study.god: God's name never appears, and the narrator refuses to soften the horror: a genocide is decreed, the enemy is handed the king's own ring, and the two men sit down to drink while a city trembles. Yet the hidden God is not idle. The lot that Haman trusts falls, by no accident, on the last month of the year, so that the very timing of the threat leaves room for a rescue. The irreversible law that dooms the Jews will, in God's hands, become the trap that dooms Haman. We learn that God governs the rage of the proud and the roll of the dice alike, and that His covenant promise to preserve His people cannot be broken, even by an empire.

crossReferences.note: The LORD declares war against Amalek from generation to generation, the ancient feud behind Haman the Agagite.
Saul spares Agag the Amalekite; centuries later a son of Kish faces an Agagite again, and the conflict is renewed.
The lot is cast into the lap, but its every decision is from the LORD, the hidden hand behind the Pur.
The dragon seeks to devour the child; the war on God's people aims ultimately at the Messiah, and always fails.

study.oneStory: An ancient, satanic hatred, wearing the face of a proud Agagite, purchases a death sentence for the entire covenant people. This is the serpent's tireless war on the seed of the woman, striving to cut off the line that leads to Christ. It looks unstoppable, sealed with the king's ring and toasted over wine. But the God who governs even the lot has already set the clock for rescue, and the promise that runs to the Messiah will not fail.

sermon.title: God Is in Charge
sermon.why: From Begg's series A Study in Esther, this message expounds Esther 3:1-15, Haman's promotion, Mordecai's refusal, and the murderous decree, holding out the sovereignty of the unseen God over even a sealed sentence of death. A faithful, expository handling of this movement.

quiz.q: What set Haman's murderous plan in motion?

options.text: Mordecai's refusal to bow, which enraged Haman and led him to target all the Jews.
options.note: Esther 3:2-6 shows Haman's rage at Mordecai widening into a plot against the whole people.

options.text: A direct command from the king to destroy the Jews.
options.note: The king only later approved Haman's scheme; the plot began with Haman's wounded pride.

options.text: A famine that Haman blamed on the Jews.
options.note: There is no famine in the account; the trigger was Mordecai's refusal to bow.

options.text: The Jews attacking Haman first.
options.note: The Jews did nothing of the kind; Haman's aggression sprang from his own pride.

quiz.q: Why is it significant that Haman is called an Agagite?

options.text: It marks him as heir of Amalek, Israel's ancient sworn enemy, renewing an old, God-ordained conflict.
options.note: Agag was king of Amalek, 1 Samuel 15; the label signals a deep, ongoing enmity.

options.text: It means he was a foreigner with no history with Israel.
options.note: The opposite: it ties him to a long history of enmity with God's people.

options.text: It simply names his home province.
options.note: It is a lineage, not a mere geographic label; it carries the Amalekite feud.

options.text: It shows he was secretly a friend of the Jews.
options.note: He is called the enemy of the Jews; the term underlines his hostility.

quiz.q: How did Haman gain the king's approval for the decree?

options.text: He slandered the Jews as lawless and offered a vast bribe; the careless king gave him the signet ring.
options.note: Esther 3:8-11 records the lie, the offered silver, and the king's ring.

options.text: He proved in court that the Jews had committed crimes.
options.note: There was no proof, only slander; the charge was a manipulative lie.

options.text: He forced the king at swordpoint.
options.note: No force was used; the king was persuaded and indifferent.

options.text: He was refused and had to act on his own.
options.note: The king granted the request and handed over his ring.

quiz.q: What does the casting of the Pur reveal, in light of the whole book?

options.text: Haman trusted chance, but God rules the lot; the date fell far off, leaving room for rescue.
options.note: Proverbs 16:33 and the plot both show God's hidden hand over the lot.

options.text: That the date was chosen by careful strategy, not chance.
options.note: It was chosen by lot; the point is God's rule over that chance.

options.text: That God had no involvement in the timing.
options.note: The narrative implies the opposite: the timing serves God's rescue.

options.text: That the Jews had already been destroyed.
options.note: The lot set a future date; the danger, and the rescue, still lay ahead.

quiz.q: How does the plot against the Jews connect to the larger biblical story?

options.text: It is another attempt in the serpent's war to destroy God's people and cut off the line to the Messiah, and it fails.
options.note: Like Pharaoh and Herod, Haman's plot is the enemy's assault on the covenant line, always defeated.

options.text: It shows that God's promises to His people had finally failed.
options.note: The opposite: the rescue proves the promises hold.

options.text: It is an isolated event with no link to the rest of Scripture.
options.note: The Amalek and serpent themes tie it deeply into the whole story.

options.text: It proves the Jews were no longer God's people.
options.note: They remain the covenant people God preserves through the crisis.

questions: Haman's genocide grew out of one bruised ego. Where do you see wounded pride in your own heart, and where could it lead if left unchecked?
Mordecai would not bow, even when it was costly and dangerous. Where is God calling you to a faithful no that the world will not understand?
The king toasted a decree of death without a second thought. How does this scene expose the casual cruelty that power and comfort can breed, and where do you need to resist it?
The lot fell exactly where God meant it to, though Haman thought it was chance. How does it comfort you that God rules even the things that feel random in your life?
Behind Haman lies an ancient hatred of God's people that has always failed. How does knowing that evil is real, but already doomed, help you face opposition to your faith?

translationNotes.note: Agagite links Haman to Agag, king of the Amalekites, 1 Samuel 15, signaling the ancient enmity between Amalek and Israel that frames the conflict.
Pur is an Akkadian loanword for the lot, which the narrator explains; the feast of Purim in chapter nine takes its name from this word.
Ten thousand talents of silver is an enormous sum, roughly 342 metric tons, underscoring the scale of Haman's obsession and bribe.
The order to destroy young and old, women and children in a single day makes the decree an act of genocide, not judicial punishment.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for Esther 3:1-15. Em dashes in verse 13 were converted to commas per house style; no dashes remain.
